Example ID: 8165771
QUESTION:
Ultrasound in squamous cell carcinoma of the penis; a useful addition to clinical staging?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] As part of the staging procedure in squamous cell carcinoma of the penis, we assessed the role of ultrasound examination, in particular its role in assessing the extent and the invasion into the corpora.
[5] Ultrasound examination in the region of the glans was not able to differentiate between invasion of the subepithelial tissue and invasion into the corpus spongiosum, but absence or presence of invasion into the tunica albuginea of the corpus cavernosum was clearly demonstrated.
[1] From 1988 until 1992, all patients referred for primary treatment underwent ultrasound assessment with a 7.5 MHz linear array small parts transducer as part of the clinical workup.
[2] All ultrasound images were reviewed by one radiologist, without knowledge of the clinical outcome and were compared with the results obtained at histopathologic examination.
[6] Accurate measurement by ultrasound of maximum tumor thickness was seen in seven of sixteen examinations.
[3] In 16 patients the primary tumor and in 1 patient a recurrent cancer after primary therapy were examined.
[4] All tumors were identified as hypoechoic lesions.

Example ID: 9003088
QUESTION:
Immunohistochemical assessment of steroid hormone receptors in tissues of the anal canal. Implications for anal incontinence?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] A prerequisite for a hormonal influence on anal continence in women is the presence of hormone receptors in the tissues of the anal canal.
[1] Using immunohistochemical techniques, we demonstrated and localized estrogen and progesterone receptors (ER, PR) in tissue sections of the anal canal.
[8] In females, about 50% of the ER-positive tissues were also found to be positive for PR.
[6] Staining for ER was detected in the perimuscular connective tissue of all females (12/12) and in four of the seven males.
[5] Estrogen receptors were found in the smooth muscle cells of the internal sphincter of all females (10/10) and in four of the seven males.
[7] No specific staining for ER or PR was found in the nuclei of striated muscle cells of the external sphincter in males or females (n = 8).
[2] Thirty-five specimens of the anorectal region from 21 patients (14 women, seven men) were examined histologically for smooth muscle (present in specimens from ten females and in seven males), striated muscle (present in three females and five males), and perimuscular connective tissue (present in 12 females and seven males).
[3] Immunostaining for ER and PR was then performed by incubating with primary anti-ER and anti-PR antibody and visualization of specific antibody binding by the ABC technique with DAB as chromogen.
[9] Amongst the male patients, only one exhibited staining for PR, and this was confined to the smooth muscle.
[4] Positive staining for ER and PR was seen exclusively over cell nuclei.

it demonstrates receptors but does not directly measure continence or incontinence outcomes.", "Receptor expression is limited or rare in some male tissues, so implications may differ by sex."], "uncertainty_flags": ["No direct clinical correlation between receptor status and anal incontinence severity or incidence is provided.", "Small sample size may limit generalizability of implications for continence."], "confidence": 0.78}

[0] In familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), correlations between site of mutation in the adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) gene and severity of colonic polyposis or extracolonic manifestations are well known.

[5] Age at diagnosis of FAP based on bowel symptoms and age at diagnosis of colorectal cancer in untreated patients were used as indicators of the severity of the natural course of the disease.
[8] Within each group of patients however large phenotypic variation was observed, even among patients with identical germline mutations.
[3] Clinical information was obtained from 1256 patients.

Example ID: 17445978
QUESTION:
Is renal warm ischemia over 30 minutes during laparoscopic partial nephrectomy possible?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] To evaluate renal damage and impairment of renal function 1 yr after laparoscopic partial nephrectomy (LPN) with warm ischemia>30 min.
[1] From July 2004 to June 2005, 18 patients underwent LPN with warm ischemia time>30 min.
[4] Renal scintigraphy was performed before the procedure, at 5 d and at 3 and 12 mo postoperatively.
[2] Kidney damage markers (daily proteinuria and tubular enzymes) and renal function (serum creatinine, cystatin C, and creatinine clearances) were assessed on postoperative days 1 and 5 and at 12 mo.
[6] In terms of kidney damage and renal function markers, the statistical analysis demonstrated that at 1 yr there was complete return to the normal range and no statistical difference between the values at the various time points.
[7] The GFR was not significantly different before and 3 mo after surgery.
[5] Statistical analysis was performed using the Student t test and logistic regression analysis.
[8] In terms of scintigraphy of the operated kidney, the values were 48.35+/-3.82% (40-50%) before the procedure, 36.88+/-8.42 (16-50%) on postoperative day 5 (p=0.0001), 40.56+/-8.96 (20-50%) at 3 mo (p=0.003), and 42.8+/-7.2% (20-50%) 1 yr after surgery (p=0.001).
[3] Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) was evaluated before surgery and at 3 mo.

Example ID: 17595200
QUESTION:
Is there an intrauterine influence on obesity?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] It has been suggested that increasing obesity levels in young women lead to intrauterine environments that, in turn, stimulate increased obesity among their offspring, generating an intergenerational acceleration of obesity levels.
[4] We carried out a sensitivity analysis to investigate the influence of non-paternity on these associations.
[1] If this mechanism is important, the association of maternal body mass index (BMI) with offspring BMI should be stronger than the association of paternal with offspring BMI.
[2] To compare the relative strengths of association of maternal and paternal BMI with offspring BMI at age 7.5, taking into account the possible effect of non-paternity.
[5] The strength of association between parental BMI and offspring BMI at age 7.5 was similar for both parents.
[3] We compared strength of association for maternal-offspring and paternal-offspring BMI for 4654 complete parent-offspring trios in the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC), using unstandardised and standardised regression analysis.
[6] Taking into account correlations between maternal and paternal BMI, performing standardised rather than unstandardised regression and carrying out a sensitivity analysis for non-paternity emphasised the robustness of the general similarity of the associations.
[7] The associations between high parental BMI (top decile) and offspring BMI are also similar for both parents.

Example ID: 28707539
QUESTION:
Visceral adipose tissue area measurement at a single level: can it represent visceral adipose tissue volume?

CANDIDATE SENTENCES:
[0] Measurement of visceral adipose tissue (VAT) needs to be accurate and sensitive to change for risk monitoring.
[1] The purpose of this study is to determine the CT slice location where VAT area can best reflect changes in VAT volume and body weight.
[4] The strongest correlations of VAT area with VAT volume and body weight changes were shown in a slice 3 cm above the lower margin of L3 with r values of 0.853 and 0.902, respectively.
[2] 60 plain abdominal CT images from 30 males [mean age (range) 51 (41-68) years, mean body weight (range) 71.1 (101.9-50.9) kg] who underwent workplace screenings twice within a 1-year interval were evaluated.
[3] Automatically calculated and manually corrected areas of the VAT of various scan levels using "freeform curve" region of interest on CT were recorded and compared with body weight changes.
